sources_audited.note: Direct fetch 403s (as the packet discloses); reopened via browser. Real May 2023 OEWS NAICS 541500 table; occupation employment and mean wages match. The '~45% of payroll in Software Developers' figure holds within the disclosed 13-occupation computer/management mix (~43% by employment x mean wage), though it is ~24% of the full all-occupation industry payroll — see caveat.

Page ranks ~800 occupations by Anthropic Economic Index observed exposure. All four cited figures verified exactly (Computer Programmers 74.5% and top-ranked; QA 51.9%; InfoSec 48.6%; User Support 46.8%); Software Developers explicitly outside top 10 with the 'more complex, collaborative, judgment-intensive' framing the packet quotes.
Eloundou et al. 'GPTs are GPTs' confirmed; used only as background framework for a, exactly as described.
All cited figures verified: Claude Code 79%/21% automation/augmentation vs Claude.ai 49%/51%; JS/TS 31%, HTML/CSS 28%, Python 14%, SQL 6%; startup ~33% vs enterprise ~13% adoption.
Background framing for rho as stated. The page's own headline split is 57% augmentation / 43% automation; the 49/51 figure the packet uses is from S4, so no misattribution.

Page contains '84.4% of programmers have tried at least one AI code generator tool' (packet phrases this as '84% using or planning to use' — slightly stronger semantics) and '82% use daily or weekly'. The 'daily-use climbing ~18% to ~73% between 2024 and 2026' trend cited in rho's rationale was not found on this page or any other registered source.

Verbatim match: AI coding tools 'have materially raised developer productivity... reducing the labor content of software development engagements', framed as a headwind for traditional development vs tailwinds for AI consulting/cybersecurity/data-cloud. Multiples history and PE buyer composition present. Does NOT contain the ~70% T&M billing-share figure used in q's rationale (see caveat).

URL is a gated download form for the 2025 Global Tech M&A Report; report contents not verifiable from the page. Corum's public insights pages corroborate the directional claims (PE tech deal value growth, sector targeting of AI/digital infrastructure/cybersecurity over traditional services), but the specific 'PE backing ~40% of tech M&A in 2024, up from ~30%' in the s5 bridge could not be verified. s5's score-driving anchor is S10, which is fully verified, and the base nets to the unadjusted anchor.
Both cited figures verified exactly: 52.3% of employer-businesses owned by people 55+, and 22% of employer-business owners plan to sell or transfer ownership within five years.
403 via direct fetch; reopened via browser. Press release verified: 40% of enterprise applications integrated with task-specific AI agents by end of 2026, up from less than 5% today.

403 via direct fetch; reopened via browser. Verified: over 40% of agentic AI projects canceled by end of 2027 (costs, unclear value, risk controls); also contains the '33% of enterprise software agentic by 2028, up from less than 1% in 2024' figure used in o.

403 via direct fetch; reopened via browser. MLR 2024-34 projections verified: Computer Systems Design and Related Services +15.8% (among 15 fastest-growing and 15 most-jobs-added industries); Software Developers +267,700 jobs, +15.8%, second-largest occupational increase.
Verified ~22% CAGR (global 22.05%, US 22.35%); packet reviewed and explicitly discounted it as a d5 anchor, which is the disclosed use.

Verified 'nearly 60% of all custom business apps are being created by employees who are not part of the IT team'; the packet's treatment of this as weakly-sourced vendor-ecosystem material is accurate and appropriately caveated.

caveats: rho: the 'daily-use rates climbing from ~18% to ~73% between 2024 and 2026' figure appears in no registered source (S6, the only adoption-survey source cited, reports 82% daily-or-weekly use with no trend series), and S6's adoption figure is 'tried at least one tool' (84.4%) rather than the packet's stronger 'using or planning to use' phrasing. Not material: rho's base rests on the fully verified S4 telemetry and S12 enterprise-friction evidence, and no principled correction of this corroborative figure moves rho's base below ~0.41, the point at which H would cross the PRIORITY L-floor.

q: the ~70% time-and-materials billing-share figure is attributed by the packet's own caveat to an industry blog aggregator that is not registered in q's source_ids; the only cited source (S7) supports the qualitative structural-headwind framing but not the 70% number. Not material: with L set by H, the base tier is insensitive to q until it falls below ~0.08.

s5: the bridge's 'PE backing ~40% of tech M&A deals in 2024, up from ~30%' statistic could not be verified — S8 is a gated report landing page, and Corum's public material corroborates only the direction. Not material: the disclosed adjustments net to the unadjusted, fully verified Gallup anchor (0.22), and F is pinned at 10.0 across the entire s5 range.

a: the 'roughly 45% of industry payroll in Software Developers' claim holds within the disclosed 13-occupation wage-weighting mix (~43%), but that mix excludes the industry's sales, office/administrative, and other non-computer occupations (~24% share of full all-occupation payroll for Software Developers); the restricted-universe choice is implicit in the bridge but adds ambiguous-direction uncertainty beyond the stated proxy error.
e: an uncited structural-judgment ESTIMATE (empty source_ids), honestly disclosed as such; no tier effect since F = 10.0 across the full e/s5 low-high range.

Dataset anchors carry disclosed weaknesses upstream of the researched primitives: l mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts, and n is margin-bridged on a generic 15.65% cross-industry EBITDA margin; both are excluded from the envelope per the stated interval_scope.

The base tier margin is thin: L = H = 4.36 against the PRIORITY floor of 4.0, so modest downward revisions to a or rho would drop the base to CONDITIONAL — consistent with the record's own CONDITIONAL-to-HIGHEST_PRIORITY interval, MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness, and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.
summary: Mechanics pass on all checks. All 14 primitive-cited sources were reopened and all are reachable; 12 support the claims cited to them (three BLS/Gartner pages 403 direct fetch but verified via browser, matching the packet's own disclosure), while S6 and S8 are partially supported. The record is honest about its evidence: every PROXY bridge names a real source population, transformation, and error direction; the four ESTIMATEs are bounded, disclosed judgment; nothing unknown is scored as zero; the lens is the default; there is no double counting (S12 supplies distinct figures to rho and o); and the driver/weakness conclusion (structurally weak value capture under T&M billing) follows directly from the verified S7 evidence rather than industry reputation — indeed the packet argues against the flattering AI-arbitrage thesis for this industry. Three quantitative claims could not be traced to registered sources (rho's daily-use trend, q's 70% T&M share, s5's PE-share statistic); each was tested for tier impact and none moves the base tier or action, because rho's base rests on verified telemetry and friction evidence, the tier is insensitive to q with L set by H, and s5 nets to the fully verified Gallup anchor with F pinned at 10. With a thin L margin above the PRIORITY floor and material estimates remaining, the appropriate reading is exactly what the record itself reports: PRIORITY base, wide CONDITIONAL-to-HIGHEST_PRIORITY interval, MODEL_CONDITIONED, VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS. Publishable with caveats.
